reference: GATE CS — Linear Algebra / Matrices and Systems

map.condition: Sum or product of all eigenvalues
Eigenvalues of A^k, A^{-1}, or f(A)

map.use: n \text{ linearly independent eigenvectors} \Leftrightarrow A = PDP^{-1}

map.condition: Rank or linear independence of eigenvectors
map.use: \text{Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are linearly independent}

formulas.name: Characteristic Equation
formulas.plain: det(A - lambda*I) = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Finding eigenvalues of any square matrix A
formulas.trap: Subtracting lambda from every element is wrong; lambda is subtracted only from the diagonal entries.

formulas.name: Eigenvector Equation
formulas.plain: A*v = lambda*v, v != 0
formulas.whenToUse: Defining or verifying an eigenvector v for eigenvalue lambda
formulas.trap: The zero vector is never an eigenvector, even though A·0 = lambda·0 is trivially true.

formulas.name: Characteristic Polynomial (2×2)
formulas.plain: p(lambda) = lambda^2 - tr(A)*lambda + det(A)
formulas.whenToUse: Quick eigenvalue computation for 2×2 matrices
formulas.trap: The sign convention: coefficient of lambda is −tr(A), not +tr(A).

formulas.name: Trace = Sum of Eigenvalues
formulas.plain: tr(A) = lambda_1 + lambda_2 + ... + lambda_n
formulas.whenToUse: Finding an unknown eigenvalue when others and the trace are known

terms.means: sum of diagonal entries of A

formulas.trap: Eigenvalues must be counted with algebraic multiplicity; a repeated root counts multiple times.

formulas.name: Determinant = Product of Eigenvalues
formulas.plain: det(A) = lambda_1 * lambda_2 * ... * lambda_n
formulas.whenToUse: Determining singularity or finding an unknown eigenvalue using the determinant
formulas.trap: If any eigenvalue is 0, the matrix is singular (det = 0). Students sometimes miss this connection.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Matrix Powers
formulas.latex: A^k v = \lambda^k v \implies \text{eigenvalues of } A^k \text{ are } \lambda^k
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of A^k are lambda^k
formulas.whenToUse: Finding eigenvalues of A^2, A^3, or any integer power of A
formulas.trap: The eigenvectors remain the same; only the eigenvalues change to lambda^k.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Inverse Matrix
formulas.latex: \text{If } A v = \lambda v \text{ and } \lambda \neq 0,\; A^{-1} v = \frac{1}{\lambda} v
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of A^{-1} are 1/lambda
formulas.whenToUse: Finding eigenvalues of the inverse; A^{-1} exists iff all eigenvalues are non-zero

terms.means: reciprocal eigenvalue for the inverse matrix

formulas.trap: A^{-1} exists only when all eigenvalues are non-zero (i.e., det(A) != 0).

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Shifted Matrix (A + cI)
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of (A + c*I) are (lambda + c)
formulas.whenToUse: When a scalar multiple of I is added to A
formulas.trap: Adding cI shifts eigenvalues but does NOT change eigenvectors.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of a Polynomial of A
formulas.latex: \text{If } p(A) = a_k A^k + \cdots + a_0 I,\text{ then eigenvalues of } p(A) \text{ are } p(\lambda)
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of p(A) are p(lambda)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding eigenvalues of expressions like A^2 - 3A + 2I

terms.means: the same polynomial evaluated at the eigenvalue

formulas.trap: This works only for polynomial functions; it does NOT directly apply to all matrix functions unless A is diagonalisable.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Transpose
formulas.plain: A and A^T share the same eigenvalues
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about eigenvalues of A^T or verifying symmetry properties
formulas.trap: Eigenvalues are the same, but eigenvectors of A^T are generally different from those of A.

formulas.name: Triangular Matrix Eigenvalues
formulas.latex: \text{Eigenvalues of upper/lower triangular } A = \text{diagonal entries } a_{11}, a_{22}, \ldots, a_{nn}
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of a triangular matrix are its diagonal entries
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever the matrix is upper or lower triangular (or diagonal)
formulas.trap: This is also true for diagonal matrices. Off-diagonal entries do NOT affect eigenvalues for triangular matrices.

formulas.name: Real Symmetric Matrix Eigenvalues
formulas.latex: A = A^T \implies \text{all eigenvalues are real and eigenvectors are orthogonal}
formulas.plain: Symmetric real matrix => real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever the matrix is symmetric (A = A^T)
formulas.trap: Even if the characteristic polynomial has repeated roots, a real symmetric matrix is always diagonalisable.

formulas.name: Idempotent Matrix Eigenvalues
formulas.plain: Idempotent matrix (A^2 = A) has eigenvalues only 0 or 1
formulas.whenToUse: Matrix satisfies A^2 = A (projection matrices)
formulas.trap: tr(A) = rank(A) for an idempotent matrix, which equals the number of eigenvalues equal to 1.

formulas.name: Nilpotent Matrix Eigenvalues
formulas.plain: Nilpotent matrix (A^k = 0) has all eigenvalues equal to 0
formulas.whenToUse: Matrix satisfies A^k = 0 for some positive integer k

terms.means: nilpotent condition; smallest such k is the nilpotency index

formulas.trap: A nilpotent matrix is not necessarily the zero matrix itself; it can have non-zero entries.

formulas.name: Orthogonal Matrix Eigenvalues
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of an orthogonal matrix have absolute value 1
formulas.whenToUse: Matrix is orthogonal (A*A^T = I); eigenvalues are ±1 or complex conjugate pairs on the unit circle
formulas.trap: Real eigenvalues of a real orthogonal matrix are restricted to +1 or −1.

formulas.name: Diagonalisation
formulas.plain: A = P*D*P^{-1}, where columns of P are eigenvectors and D is diagonal of eigenvalues
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing A in diagonal form; computing A^k efficiently as P D^k P^{-1}

terms.means: matrix whose columns are n linearly independent eigenvectors

formulas.trap: Diagonalisation requires n linearly independent eigenvectors. A matrix with a repeated eigenvalue may or may not be diagonalisable.

formulas.name: Condition for Diagonalisability
formulas.latex: A \text{ is diagonalisable} \iff \text{geometric multiplicity} = \text{algebraic multiplicity for each } \lambda
formulas.plain: Diagonalisable iff geometric multiplicity = algebraic multiplicity for every eigenvalue
formulas.whenToUse: Checking if a matrix can be diagonalised, especially when eigenvalues repeat

terms.means: power of (lambda - lambda_i) in characteristic polynomial
dimension of null space of (A - lambda_i * I)

formulas.trap: Geometric multiplicity is always <= algebraic multiplicity. Equality is required for diagonalisability.

formulas.name: Cayley–Hamilton Theorem
formulas.plain: Every matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial: p(A) = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Reducing high powers of A, finding A^{-1}, or simplifying matrix polynomial expressions

terms.means: same polynomial with lambda replaced by the matrix A

formulas.trap: Substituting eigenvalues into p(A) gives 0; that's not the theorem. The theorem says the matrix A itself satisfies p(A) = 0.

formulas.name: Distinct Eigenvalues => Linearly Independent Eigenvectors
formulas.latex:
\lambda_1 
eq \lambda_2 
eq \cdots 
eq \lambda_k \implies v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k 	ext{ are linearly independent}
formulas.plain: Eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are linearly independent
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying that the set of eigenvectors forms a basis (when eigenvalues are all distinct)
formulas.trap: The converse is false: linearly independent eigenvectors can correspond to the same eigenvalue (repeated eigenvalue case).

formulas.name: Eigenspace (Null Space Definition)
formulas.plain: Eigenspace E_lambda = null space of (A - lambda*I)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding all eigenvectors for a given eigenvalue (solve (A - lambda*I)v = 0)

terms.means: set of all eigenvectors for lambda, plus the zero vector

formulas.trap: The geometric multiplicity equals dim(E_lambda), NOT the algebraic multiplicity (unless all eigenvalues are distinct).
